coredata.dc:title: Effect of Ti/B on fabricating TiB2p/AZ91 composites by employing a TiB2p/Al master alloy
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Y.
Wang, H.Y.
Ma, B.X.
Xiu, K.
Jiang, Q.C.
coredata.dc:description: The TiB2 particulate reinforced magnesium matrix composites (MMCs) were fabricated by adding a TiB2–Al master alloy processed via self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) reaction of Al–Ti–B systems with the different molar ratio of Ti to B (n Ti:n B =1:2 and n Ti:n B =1:2.5, respectively) to molten magnesium and using the semisolid slurry stirring technique. When the molar ratio of Ti to B is 1:2 in TiB2–Al master alloy, the TiB2–Al master alloy and TiB2p/AZ91 composites contained a certain amount of transient TiAl3 phase; however, when the molar ratio of Ti to B is 1:2.5, the TiAl3 phase was almost completely eliminated and the distribution of TiB2 particulates generally appeared to be more homogeneous throughout the magnesium matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Al–Ti–B
SHS
Particulates
Magnesium
Composites

TiB2–Al Master alloys with the different Ti/B
TiB2p/AZ91 composite fabricated by using the Al–Ti–B system (n Ti:n B =1:2.5)

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The data denotes the master alloy containing 50wt.% Al, balanced with Ti and B powders (the molar ratio of Ti to B is 1:2).

row.entry: TiB2–Al master alloys with different Ti/B

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermodynamic data of TiAl3 and Al

article.ce:title: Effect of Ti/B on fabricating TiB2p/AZ91 composites by employing a TiB2p/Al master alloy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials of Ministry of Education and Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University at Nanling Campus, No. 142 Renmin Street, Changchun 130025, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The TiB2 particulate reinforced magnesium matrix composites (MMCs) were fabricated by adding a TiB2–Al master alloy processed via self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) reaction of Al–Ti–B systems with the different molar ratio of Ti to B (n Ti:n B =1:2 and n Ti:n B =1:2.5, respectively) to molten magnesium and using the semisolid slurry stirring technique. When the molar ratio of Ti to B is 1:2 in TiB2–Al master alloy, the TiB2–Al master alloy and TiB2p/AZ91 composites contained a certain amount of transient TiAl3 phase; however, when the molar ratio of Ti to B is 1:2.5, the TiAl3 phase was almost completely eliminated and the distribution of TiB2 particulates generally appeared to be more homogeneous throughout the magnesium matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: Particulate reinforced magnesium metal matrix composites (MMCs) have a great potential to be applied in automobile and aerospace industries because of their high specific tensile strength and modulus, as well as their high wear resistance [1,2]. There are several methods to fabricate particulate reinforced Mg MMCs including stir casting [2,3], mechanical alloying (MA) [4], powder metallurgy (P/M) [5–7], squeeze cast [8], infiltration [9] and self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) [10]. In above mentioned methods, those allowing in situ production of the reinforcement offer some significant advantages: (a) the in situ formed reinforcements are thermodynamically stable at the matrix, leading to less degradation in elevated-temperature service, (b) the interfaces between reinforcement and matrix are clean, resulting in a strong interfacial bonding, and (c) the in situ formed reinforcing particulates are finer in size and their distribution in the matrix is more uniform, yielding better mechanical properties [11]. Compared with the other in situ techniques, SHS is extremely attractive, its main advantages being the self-generation of energy required for the process, the high-purity products due to volatilization of low boiling point impurity at elevated temperature, and high productivity due to very high reaction rates [12]. However, there are some unfavorable transient phases in the final product due to the high reaction rate and high cooling rate of the SHS reaction [10].

Two types of reinforcing particulates have been investigated for magnesium matrix composites. The first and most widely used is ceramic. The other is metallic/intermetallic [13]. Among the various ceramic reinforcements, SiC is the widely used because its relatively better wettability and its stability in magnesium melt [2,14]. In recent years, TiC and B4C have received more attention in particulate reinforced Mg composite [6,15–17]. Titanium diboride (TiB2), possessing outstanding features such as high melting point (2790°C), high hardness (33GPa), high modulus (530GPa) and low density (4.451g/cm3) [18,19], is another potential reinforcement in Mg composites. However, investigation on Mg MMCs reinforced with TiB2 particulate is rather limited [6,10,20]. The unfavorable brittle Al3Ti phase, usually very large in size, was always present in TiB2 particulates reinforced Mg composites fabricated by SHS reaction of Al–Ti–B system [10,20], which may degrade plasticity of the composites. At present, there is very little information available on how to eliminate the brittle TiAl3 phase in Mg composite fabricated by SHS reaction of Al–Ti–B system. Wang et al. [20] added about 5wt.% C powders into the Al–Ti–B system and successfully eliminate the TiAl3 phase in TiB2/AZ91 composite, but about 25wt.% TiC particulates were also synthesized in the Al–Ti–B systems. The purpose of this study is to find, based on the study of TiB2–Al master alloy, a method of precluding the transient TiAl3 phase in order to fabricate high quality Mg MMCs with monolithic TiB2 reinforced particulates.

Al (99.0 purity, ∼29μm), Ti (99.5 purity, ∼15μm), and B (98.0 purity, ∼3μm) were mixed by ball milling for 8h in a stainless steel container according to Table 1 . Then each powder mixture with a different molar ratio of Ti to B was mechanically pressed into a cylindrical preform at pressures ranging from 20 to 25MPa. Each cylindrical preform (58mm diameter and about 53mm height) with about 70% theoretical density was heated in a closed electronic resistance furnace of 3kW, which was first evacuated and then filled with high purity Ar gas at atmospheric pressure. At about 680–720°C, a SHS reaction of Al–Ti–B system occurred and TiB2p–Al master alloy was then formed.

Processing of the TiB2 particulate reinforced Mg composite consists of adding the TiB2–Al master alloy to molten magnesium, semi-solid slurry stirring, and pouring. Based on the chemical compositions of Mg-9 pct Al-1 pct Zn alloy AZ91, commercially pure Mg, Al and Zn were selected to prepare the matrix alloy. About 700g matrix alloy was heated to 750°C and held at that temperature for 20min in an electric resistance furnace containing a graphite crucible under SF6/CO2 protective atmosphere. And then the desired amount of TiB2–Al master alloy was subsequently added into the magnesium melt and held at that temperature until the TiB2–Al master alloy was dissolved. Stirring was carried out in the semi-solid temperature range for 20min, and then the composite slurry was heated to 750°C and then was poured into a preheated steel mould with 55mm diameter and 180mm height.

Microstructure and phase analyses of the master alloy and composite were investigated by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Model JSM-5310, Japan) equipped with energy-dispersive spectrum (EDS) (Model Link-Isis, Britain) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Model D/Max 2500PC Rigaku, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TiB2–Al master alloy and TiB2p/AZ composite
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM fractograph and XRD pattern of the TiB2–Al master alloy synthesized by a 50wt.% Al–Ti–B (n Ti:n B =1:2) system are shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), respectively. XRD result shows that main components of the obtained master alloy are TiB2 and Al, but a little amount of TiAl3 is also detected. Compared to the Al–Ti–C system, investigations on the Al–Ti–B system are limited. Although the Al–Ti–B system has been adopted to fabricate in situ TiB2 particle reinforced Al-based composites by several researchers [21,22], existing studies [11] agree that there is a lack of understanding of the reaction mechanisms, which hinders the optimization of the process and the TiB2–Al product.

During SHS reaction of Al–Ti–B system, the chemical reaction of the process is essential, because it is of interest to help to understand the reaction mechanisms of the system. Therefore, it is necessary to study the thermodynamics of the system to determine the possibility of some reactions during the in situ synthesis process. During the in situ reaction, the following three reactions are possible to occur:
(1)
3 Al + Ti → TiA
l 3
,
Δ
G
° 1
= − 37023 + 13.533 T
J / mol
(2)
TiA
l 3
+ 2 B → Ti
B 2
+ 3 Al,
Δ
G
° 2
= − 247477 + 6967 T
J / mol
(3)
Ti + 2 B → Ti
B 2
,
Δ
G
° 3
= − 284500 + 20.5 T
J / mol
Using the thermodynamic data from [23], the change of standard Gibbs free energy G° for each reaction is calculated and shown in Fig. 2 as a function of temperature. It can be observed that the changes in standard Gibbs free energy of three reactions are all negative, which indicates that the above reactions are all favorable. It is worth noting that
G
° 3
is more negative for the formation of TiB2 in the temperature range of interest, and thus reaction (3) has a higher tendency for the formation of TiB2 than the reaction (2). Therefore, it can be deduced that the final equilibrium phases should be TiB2 and Al in the SHS products of Al–Ti–B system. However, near the melting point of aluminium, the reaction for TiAl3 formation is more favorable, kinetically, and hence occurs initially. This proposal is consistent with the experimental results of Brinkman et al. [24]. The heat generated by the formation of the TiAl3 phase may subsequently initiate the reactions between TiAl3 or Ti and B, which may be the cause of the ignition temperature of the Al–Ti–B system between 680 and 720°C. SHS reaction possesses high cooling rate and the higher the adiabatic temperature, the higher the cooling rate [12]. Comparing with the Al–Ti–C system, the adiabatic temperature of SHS reaction of Al–Ti–B system is higher due to the formation heat releases of TiB2
(
Δ
H
Ti
B 2
= − 324
kJ / mol )
being higher than that of TiC (ΔH TiC =−184kJ/mol) [25]. Therefore, some transient TiAl3, which did not have enough time to further react, remained and some transient TiAl3 were found in the final TiB2–Al master alloy.

Fig. 3 (a)–(c) show the SEM microstructure and XRD pattern of the Mg composite fabricated by adding TiB2–Al (n Ti:n B =1:2) master alloy, respectively. From the XRD results, the composite consists of Mg, Mg17Al12 and TiB2, indicating that TiB2 is relatively stable in magnesium. It should be mentioned that the coarse TiAl3 phases are also found as expected in the composite, as shown in Fig. 3(a), since they have been found in the master alloy. The EDS analysis identified that long-strip shape and blocky shape phase is TiAl3, and the TiAl3 phase cannot be confirmed by XRD (shown in Fig. 1(b)) due to its low intensity. The large brittle TiAl3 phase may have harmful effects on the properties of the composite. From Fig. 3(a) and (c), it can be seen that some agglomeration of TiB2 particulates, leading to MMCs with poor mechanical strength and toughness [19], could be observed and the agglomeration is even worse around the long-strip and blocky shape TiAl3 phase. The reason for some agglomeration of TiB2 in the Mg composite may be that although some TiB2 particulates were synthesized by the reaction (2), some of them were still combined with the unreacted portion of the TiAl3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TiB2–Al Master alloys with the different Ti/B
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a)–(d) show the SEM fractograph and XRD pattern of the master alloys synthesized by a 50wt.% Al–Ti–B (n Ti:n B =1:2.2) system and by a 50wt.% Al–Ti–B (n Ti:n B =1:2.5) system, respectively. From the SEM fractograph, it can be seen that the size of the TiB2 particulates in Fig. 4(a) and (c) are almost the same as that in Fig. 1(a), which is not consistent with the result of Taneoka and Odawara [26]. The reason for this difference is that high amount of Al “inert” matrix (i.e. 50wt.%), rather than slightly superfluous B powder, play a leading role in determining the adiabatic temperature of the Al–Ti–B system in this study and the grain growth of the reaction product is an exponential function of the combustion temperature [12]. From the XRD patterns in Fig. 1(b), Fig. 4(b) and (d), it can be seen that as the molar ratio of Ti to B decreases, the intensity of TiB2 (100) peaks is observed to increase and the intensity of TiAl3 (112) reduce; furthermore, the TiAl3 (112) peak appears to diminish as the molar ratio of Ti to B decrease to 1:2.5.

To reflect a clearer comparison, the relative fractions [20,22,27] of Al:TiB2:TiAl3 in the master alloys synthesized by the starting materials with the different molar ratio of Ti to B are calculated based on the intensity of the Al (111), TiB2 (100) and TiAl3 (112) diffraction peaks, as shown in Table 2 . From Table 2, it can be seen that, as the molar ratio of Ti to B decreased, the relative fraction of TiB2 and TiAl3 are increased and reduced, respectively.

Let us assume an infinite small TiAl3 system. Considering there is not enough time to dissipate the heat generated by producing TiAl3 in the molten Al, Eq. (1) has completed, hence the system can be regarded as an adiabatic system, i.e. the heat generated by the formation of TiAl3 goes only to raise the temperature of TiAl3 and there is no loss of heat to the surroundings. T 0 is taken as 973K according to our study.

Table 3 lists thermodynamic data of TiAl3 and Al from [23]. Based on the thermodynamic data in Table 3, the following equation can be calculated:
(4)
Δ
H
°
f (
T 0
)
=
Δ
H
°
( 298 K )
+
∫
298 K
T 0
Δ
c p
d t − 3
Δ
H
m , Al
(5)
−
Δ
H
°
f (
T 0
)
>
∫
T 0
T m
c p
d T +
Δ
H
m , TiA
l 3
where T 0 is the reactive temperature of TiAl3 (i.e. T 0 =973K) and T m the melting point of TiAl3;
Δ
H
°
f ( 298 K )
and
Δ
H
°
f (
T 0
)
the standard formation enthalpy and formation enthalpy of TiAl3 at T 0, respectively, ΔH m,Al and
Δ
H
m , TiA
l 3
the enthalpy of fusion of Al and TiAl3, respectively, c p the heat capacity of solid TiAl3, and Δc p is the difference between the heat capacities of the product and reactants.

The above thermodynamic calculation result shows that the heat generated by the formation of TiAl3 make TiAl3 to be wholly melted. But the whole melt of TiAl3 required a definite time kinetically. In initial stage, some boron atom can diffuse into the molten TiAl3 portion to form some TiB2 particulates by the reaction (2) (shown in Fig. 2) and then some boron atoms can react with Ti atoms by the reaction (3) (also shown in Fig. 2). As n Ti/n B decrease, the concentration of B in solution became higher, and hence more boron atoms diffuse further into the molten TiAl3 portion to form more TiB2 particulates and more B atom reacts with Ti atoms to form more TiB2 particulates. The result, that the relative fraction of Al is increased with the decreasing of molar ratio of Ti to B, also confirms that the reaction between TiAl3 and B really occurred. Therefore, the amount of TiB2 particulates increases with the decreasing of molar ratio of Ti to B and TiAl3 was almost eliminated in the final master alloy when n Ti/n B =1:2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TiB2p/AZ91 composite fabricated by using the Al–Ti–B system (n Ti:n B =1:2.5)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (a)–(c) show the SEM microstructure and XRD pattern of the Mg composite fabricated by adding TiB2–Al (n Ti:n B =1:2.5) master alloy. From the XRD results, the composite also consists of Mg, Mg17Al12 and TiB2. Compared with microstructure of the Mg composite fabricated by adding TiB2–Al (n Ti:n B =1:2) master alloy (shown in Fig. 3(a) and (c)), the as-cast microstructure of the composite reveals a relatively uniform distribution of TiB2 particulate and no agglomeration of TiB2 particulates are observed. The shape of TiB2 particulate is hexagonal or rectangular with a size of ∼3.0μm, as shown in Fig. 5(c). Furthermore, the long-strip or blocky shape TiAl3 phase cannot be found in the SEM microstructure, since it has almost been eliminated in the master alloys. In this study, TiB2 particulates were in situ formed in Al via SHS reaction, and thus the surface of TiB2 is coated with Al and is uncontaminated, and this has significantly improved the wetting of the TiB2 in the Mg alloy. Therefore, the bonding between TiB2 particulate and Mg matrix may be good.

ce:list-item.ce:para: When the molar ratio of Ti to B is 1:2, the transient TiAl3 phase is unavoidably found in TiB2–Al master alloy and in TiB2/AZ91 composite fabricated by using the master alloy, and some agglomeration of TiB2 particulates in the Mg composite could be observed.

The transient TiAl3 phase can be almost eliminated in TiB2–Al master alloy and in TiB2/AZ91 composite fabricated by using a master alloy with n Ti:n B =1:2.5 in the starting mixture, and the distribution of TiB2 appeared to be more homogeneous throughout the magnesium matrix.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by The National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 50371030 and 50531030) and the project 985-Automotive Engineering of Jilin University as well as The Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China (No. 2005CCA00300).
